data.title: The battle over ‘mad’ plans to redevelop Liverpool Street station
data.description:
On a cold night this week as the rain lashed down, campaigners gathered in an unassuming east London office block to thwart a £1.5bn revamp of Britain’s busiest station.

The Liverpool Street Station Campaign (Lissca) – a “supergroup” of heritage enthusiasts spearheaded by TV presenter Griff Rhys Jones – is fighting plans to demolish and rebuild key parts of the railway terminus and linked Underground station.

The group this week unveiled its alternative vision, one they claim will help to modernise Liverpool Street station without destroying its history.

The rival plan drawn up by John McAslan + Partners, the architects behind the transformation of King’s Cross station in 2012, will be presented to local officials in the coming weeks.

“If you’re coming from Italy on a train to Liverpool Street station, would you rather see a brand new gateway ... or something which says Victorian, Britain and great railway heritage?” Rhys Jones says.

“If you break it, it’s gone, you can’t get it back. It could be a much richer experience, without doing an awful lot.”

Network Rail say expansion is needed to cope with the expected increase in passenger numbers at a station already at capacity. They argue only a wholesale overhaul will do.

Campaigners, meanwhile, claim that plans to modernise the station would ruin a Victorian landmark and create years of needless disruption.

The fight over Liverpool Street station, which opened fully in 1875, is a microcosm of the tug of war between heritage and modernisation that threatens to hamper Labour’s ambitions for a construction boom.

Since taking power, Sir Keir Starmer has vowed to make it more difficult for people to oppose major building projects.

He has pledged to override the “whims of Nimbys” and back the builders rather than the “blockers”. But in cases like Liverpool Street, these statements have only galvanised the opposition.

Al Watson, head of planning at Taylor Wessing, says: “The situation is more tense now than it was four years ago because the previous government leaned more towards heritage.

“This Government, by its very nature, pre- and post-election, is pro-development. That’s the vision, that’s the instruction, and that’s why it is more tense now than it has been in a long time.”

Until now, Lissca has faced down each proposal to redevelop Liverpool Street station. The group in fact dates back to the 1970s, when British Rail’s initial bid to demolish the terminus was thwarted by Lissca, then fronted by the poet Sir John Betjeman.

More recently, plans drawn up in 2023 by architects Herzog and de Meuron were shot down after the group helped mobilise 2,200 objections and 26,000 petition signatures.

“We’ve won battle after battle,” says Sir Simon Jenkins, founder of the Railway Heritage Trust and a supporter of the group.

The new plans, ordered by Network Rail and designed by architects Acme, will involve partially demolishing the station to expand its concourse.

A 19-storey, 97m glass office tower would be built above a neighbouring listed former hotel, while gothic yellow brick vaults would arch over the station’s entrances.

Over 150 million passengers by 2041

Network Rail argues that expansion is essential.

The opening of the Elizabeth Line, which passes through Liverpool Street, has helped the station become the UK’s busiest in each of the past three years.

There were 98 million rail entries and exits last year, and Network Rail predicts this will grow to 158 million by 2041.

Planning officials for the City of London Corporation are currently reviewing the revamp proposals, with councillors expected to vote on them on Feb 10.

The scheme has so far attracted more than 2,275 letters of objection and 1,135 in favour.

The February deadline has galvanised Lissca into action, with the group hoping their alternative plans will convince the City of London to reject Network Rail’s plans.

“What we have to do over the next 20 days … is do what we can to make sure that [the committee is] aware that the City of London deserves better than this current scheme that is before them, and they have the right to ask for it to be better,” Jones says.

‘Illogical’ plans

While Network Rail’s designs will evoke the station’s Victorian heritage, Lissca believe they can retain far more of the original building.

They estimates its plans will take half the time and cost half as much as Network Rail’s proposals, with almost no demolition required.

“The reason it’s so high is because of the void costs, created by the demolition of so much fabric,” says McAslan. “It’s so illogical, it’s mad.”

Lissca’s vision includes a green walkway inspired by the Eden Project, with Sir Tim Smit, founder of the Eden Project, advising McAslan on landscape strategy.

A vaulted office building would sit at the northern end of the station’s platforms, with nine floors of timber hanging from a lightweight steel frame to provide around 700,000 sq ft of workspace.

McAslan believes Network Rail’s plans will cause 12 years of disruption for travellers – a timeframe he calls “completely unacceptable”.

But Network Rail says this timeframe is a gross exaggeration.

Work on modernising the station itself will begin as early as 2028 and be completed in about five years, with development of offices above the passenger concourse taking three more.

While ticket barriers will be moved and shops demolished to enlarge the concourse, the impact on people using the station will be limited to the “mild inconvenience” of having to use different entrances at certain times, Network Rail says.

Officials argue the project should be no more disruptive than the modernisation of Paddington at the turn of the century and far removed from the upheaval that accompanied the upgrade of London Bridge station.

The five-year, £1bn project to transform London Bridge, the capital’s oldest rail terminus, resulted in delays and dangerous levels of overcrowding.

A Network Rail spokesman said: “It’s very much not going to take 12 years and it won’t be on the scale of London Bridge because none of the work being done is to create new tracks and platforms.”

Network Rail commissioned an independent team to review McAslan’s preliminary scheme but concluded it was undeliverable owing to complexity, funding challenges and the potential for prolonged station closures.

Its review concluded that building offices over the train shed – as proposed in the McAslan plan – would be too costly and structurally challenging because Network Rail does not own land to either side.

It would also require “significant closure” of the station to build a deck over live lines.

Back in the war room at McAslan’s Farringdon offices, campaigners are undeterred.

“There’s a critical mass of buildings which create a sense of the Victorian past in that part of the city, and it must be preserved,” says Sir Simon.

“Just remember, there’s always a choice. Nothing is inevitable. Fight on.”

data.title: Florence flats so ugly they’ve triggered a police investigation
data.description:
Not far from the banks of the Arno River, an ultra-modern block of upmarket flats nicknamed the Black Cube casts a gloomy shadow over the butter and cream-coloured villas lining the embankment.

“Brutto [ugly],” says resident Stefano Pieri, shaking his head as he peered across the river at the dark, burnished brass facade of the building in the heart of Florence. “Some might say it is beautiful modern architecture when it stands alone. The problem is it is completely out of context here.”

The building is indeed out of place – so much so it is now subject to a police investigation, dragging in everyone from architects to councillors. Even the city’s nobility have intervened, breaking cover to stop any further developments from spoiling a skyline that has changed little in hundreds of years.

The Black Cube – officially called the Teatro Luxury Apartments – is a stone’s throw from the US embassy and the Santa Maria Novella train station, and includes smart restaurants, spas and underground parking. The modern palazzo, managed by the Star Hotels chain, offers 150 short-term rental units for high-income students and professionals.

Florentine prosecutors are now questioning 12 people who are under investigation for irregularities in the permitting, planning and zoning process that paved the way for the Black Cube to rise high above the former site of the Teatro Comunale. They are trying to trace how the property was decommissioned and sold in 2013 by the then Matteo Renzi city administration.

What they know so far is that the property passed through a state entity then was auctioned off to an Italian investment subsidiary. From there it was sold to a joint venture formed by property giants Blue Noble and Hines, within the Future Living fund managed by London-based Savills. Hines maintains it constructed the building with all the necessary permits in place.

Here in the centre of the Renaissance city of the 14th-century Palazzo Vecchio, Giotto’s Campanile and Brunelleschi’s Dome, residents cannot even change the shade of their window shutters without an extensive bureaucratic permit process.

People are now questioning just how a US property developer was granted permission to build something locals also refer to as The Monster of Corso Italia.

A powerful group of ancient noble families has called for a moratorium on further high-end developments down river from the Black Cube in a former industrial area near the former Leopolda railway station.

In a letter to the mayor, the 16 noble families said: “We must save Florence from any other ‘black cubes’. No more violence toward our city.” Some of the letter’s signatory families have owned property in the city since the Medici era (1434 to 1737).

Convincing them to speak out has been called Operation Blue Blood by local media, and is largely thanks to a lobbying effort by Roberto Budini Gattai, an architect nicknamed the Communist Nobleman, who has been at the forefront of the protests.

According to Italian media, the letter of protest was drafted over an elegant lunch, where the members of the ancient families finally agreed to sign up to the cause. Among the signatories is Claude Marie Agnès Cathérine d’Orléans, 82, a French princess and former Duchess of Aosta, who married Prince Amedeo of Savoy in 1964 and has lived in Florence ever since.

The Princess is not one to mince her words. “The Black Cube and the Social Hub on Viale Belfiore are monstrosities,” she told the Corriere Fiorentino. “I apologise to the architects who designed them, but that’s the way it is. I don’t understand why, when you do something new, you don’t try – I don’t mean copying – but perhaps taking inspiration from older buildings.”

It is the question on everyone’s lips here. “Its obviously not for Florentines,” said Jacopo Palorni, 37, as he walked along the riverbank with his wife and toddler in a stroller.

Palorni and Maria Christina Silvestre are among many residents who have been forced to move out of the historic centre of Florence and find somewhere more affordable on the outskirts. Like many, they feel their concerns about the urban future of their city go mostly unheeded. They said: “When they began construction we sent several emails, but never got any response.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Shock in the City as Schroders family cash out after 222 years
data.description:
Just several months ago, Richard Oldfield was adamant – Schroders, the 222-year-old fund manager founded during the Napoleonic Wars, was not for sale.

The Schroders family, who hold a 44pc stake in the business, were “tremendously supportive” of the business, and Mr Oldfield, who took over as chief executive in November 2024, was insistent that the company would deliver on the rest of his transformation plan.

Even as rumours swirled that the dynasty could finally cash out, the firm maintained that the dozen or so Schroder family members who own the stake intend to retain “a substantial shareholding in the company over the long term”.

But on Thursday, all of that changed. In a major shock to the City, Schroders announced that it had succumbed to a £10bn takeover from US funds group Nuveen – ending the Schroder family’s two centuries-long association with the City of London.

The change of heart emerged just a few weeks ago, when the fund manager received its first takeover approach from Nuveen.

Secret talks between the two sides about a deal – which was codenamed “Project Pantheon” – progressed rapidly, with the pair operating in secret under the code names “Aphrodite” and “Zeus” to keep the merger under wraps in the notoriously gossipy City.

Lazard, a similarly esteemed old City name, was drafted in to advise the Schroders family’s Principal Shareholder Group vehicle – with agreement from senior members of the clan finally reached in recent days.

The family, who have been a force in the City of London broadly since its creation, stand to make around £4.3bn from the sale, which will likely mark the end of their involvement in the firm.

The sale is a blow for the City and marks the end of one of the longest and most distinguished chapters in British finance, with only the Rothschilds or Warburgs on par with the Schroders for historical City significance.

It’s not the first time that the Schroder family has conceded defeat to Wall Street.

In 2000, the family – then led by Bruno Schroder and his brother-in-law George von Mallinckrodt – sold its merchant bank to Citigroup for £1.35bn after accepting it could never compete with the balance sheet of America’s most powerful banks.

The family’s involvement in Schroders has shrunk in the ensuing years. Philip Mallinckrodt, the last family member to be an executive at the company, left the board in 2020.

However, Leonie Schroder, who owns Hurstbourne Park, a 485-hectare Hampshire estate, and is the daughter of Bruno Schroder, along with Claire Fitzalan Howard, the daughter of George von Mallinckrodt, still sit on the company’s board but have little involvement with the day-to-day operations.

Just like at the turn of the millennium, Schroders has found itself outgunned by their bigger American rivals. Mr Oldfield said that the Nuveen takeover would give it the heft to take on its much greater Wall Street rivals.

“It isn’t that we had to do this,” Mr Oldfield said. “What became really clear is, as we started to get to know Nuveen, the complementarity of this combination accelerates what we could do on our own by a decade.

“In an industry that is changing and consolidating quite rapidly, this actually is a phenomenal ability to put us in the driving seat in the industry. If we were on our own, we would be less able to do that.”

For ex-employees at Schroders, Thursday’s sale is bittersweet.

Richard Buxton, who spent 12 years at the firm, said that he had been bombarded with messages from friends in the City describing the takeover as the end of an era.

“In a way it is, of course; they’ve been listed here an awfully long time,” he said.

He said the declining influence of the family owners, combined with its powerful brand, meant it was always a likely takeover candidate.

“Ultimately, the family ceased to have any management involvement,” he said. “It just looks such an obvious thing to happen. I’m not surprised at all.”

Like many British asset managers, Schroders has found itself battling a number of unfavourable headwinds, such as the increasing draw to the United States and its mega-cap tech stocks.

Ben Williams, an analyst at Shore Capital, said, “If you look at funds investing in UK equities, the data is moderately horrific, because UK funds have been in outflows forever, and that’s been a significant part of what has suppressed the valuation of these businesses and opened up this gap.”

At the same time, the popularity of passive investment into more affordable index and exchange-traded funds has grown inexorably.

With $2.5tn (£1.8tn) in assets under management, the combined group would rival giants such as Capital Group, the world’s largest active-only investor, which has around $3tn in assets.

Since taking over as chief executive, Mr Oldfield has axed a number of the group’s businesses, ending a joint venture with Lloyds Bank and exiting smaller operations in places such as Brazil and Indonesia.

While the share price has risen 28pc over his short tenure, it has done little to address the fundamental challenges facing the business.

“Many strong franchises in the UK are trading at a discount to intrinsic value and as a result are attracting interest from corporate and private capital acquirers,” a fund manager at a rival asset manager admitted.

Arguably, Schroders has been slow to grow its private markets business, where fees are higher and investors are more long-term.

Nuveen’s strength in the sector will help address the weakness, and the combined group will have a private markets franchise with more than $414bn in assets.

The takeover will not consign the Schroders brand to history. It will be retained by Nuveen, and the group’s London office will be its largest by headcount. Cost-cutting does not appear to be front of mind, either.

“We’ve been very clear that this is not a cost synergy transaction,” William Huffman, Nuveen’s chief executive, said. “This is about growth.”

Schroders said that Nuveen, which is privately owned, had committed to a dual listing on the London Stock Exchange if it went public in the future, but there was no commitment that London would be the main home of a publicly listed Nuveen.

For now, Schroders will join a growing list of public British companies swallowed up by American investors, including Darktrace, the British cybersecurity firm, and car parts group Dowlais.

The UK’s shrinking public markets once concerned the boss of Schroders. At an industry conference less than four months ago, he argued that listed companies were vital for transparency and holding management to account.

“We have to be careful to call the death of public markets ... They are super important to our futures,” he said at the time.

However, Mr Oldfield was adamant that, in any case, his deal did not represent a blow to the City of London.

“We’re committed to London. We’re committed to helping people in London. We are committed to supporting infrastructure investment across the UK,” he said.

“If anyone thinks this is a step back from the UK or a step back from London, they haven’t really read the details of the deal.”

data.title: Leon cuts hundreds of jobs in turnaround drive
data.description:
Leon has cut 244 jobs since collapsing into administration as founder John Vincent seeks to turn around the fast food chain’s fortunes.

An update from administrators Quantuma Advisory shows that Leon has shut 22 underperforming stores since December, when the business entered administration. It leaves Leon with 573 staff.

The administrators’ report lays bare the scale of Leon’s losses, which totalled £12.5m in 2023, £8.3m in 2024, and almost £10m on draft figures for 2025.

Leon had net liabilities of £8.4m at the time of administration, with an unsecured debt pile of well over £15m once trade suppliers, rent and outstanding loans are taken into account.

HMRC alone is owed some £2.5m, which administrators expect to pay in full if a proposed company voluntary agreement (CVA) to rescue the company is successful.

Mr Vincent co-founded Leon as a healthy fast food chain in 2004, alongside Henry Dimbleby and Allegra McEvedy. The chain was sold to Mohsin and Zuber Issa’s EG Group in 2021 and later became part of Asda. Mr Vincent bought the company back from Asda last October but almost immediately ran into difficulties.

He previously blamed Labour’s “incredibly toxic tax regime” for making it harder for hospitality businesses to turn a profit.

In January, he told the BBC’s Big Boss Interview podcast that the Government takes “36p of every pound” Leon makes, leaving the business with “negative 10p” once VAT, business rates, National Insurance and other costs are factored in.

A trading update for the period between Dec 10 and Jan 30 shows Leon generated around £4.5m in sales, franchise royalties and lease premiums. However, the chain still suffered losses of £2.7m once staff, food, rent and other overheads were included.

Leon is going through a CVA for the second time after a pandemic downturn forced a restructuring by Asda owner EG Group in 2020.

Quantuma Advisory also noted that under Asda’s ownership, the brand notched up losses of over £60m before the buyback.

The firm hopes to use cash from future trading to pay back its creditors, rather than rely on a fire sale of assets.

Leon was approached for comment.

data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.

data.title: BP scraps £550m shareholder payout in scramble to cut debts
data.description:
BP has pledged to “do better” after cancelling a $750m (£549m) shareholder payout as it scrambles to cut debts.

The FTSE 100 giant, which helps fund the pensions of millions of Britons through dividends, scrapped its share buyback programme on Tuesday after battening down the hatches following turbulence at the group.

It will seek to cut billions more in costs in 2026 alongside scrapping the buyback to reduce its debt pile 

The oil giant is struggling under the weight of a $22bn debt mountain and has suffered recent turmoil following the sudden exit of its chief executive Murray Auchincloss after less than two years in post.

Albert Manifold, BP’s chairman who was instrumental in Mr Auchincloss’s exit, has hired Woodside Energy chief Meg O’Neill as his replacement.

On Tuesday, Carol Howle, BP’s interim chief executive, attempted to placate investors by saying the company would seek to improve its performance.

“We are in action and we can and will do better for our shareholders,” she said.

BP’s latest $750m buyback programme was announced in November 2025 before Tuesday’s suspension.

BP said it had sunk to a $3.4bn loss in the three months ending in December, compared to a $1.2bn profit in the third quarter of 2025, amid weaker crude oil prices.

Its underlying replacement costs profit – its preferred measure of profitability – fell from $8.9bn to $7.5bn for the year. Its replacement costs profit fell 16pc over the quarter, in line with analysts forecasts.

The latest fall in profitability included an impairment charge of $4bn on its “low carbon energy” division, part of a costly write-down of BP’s net zero bets disclosed in January.

BP had expected the oil price to be around $76 a barrel for this year but it currently trades at around $69, adding pressure to the company’s finances.

“With a continued emphasis on capital discipline and returns, we are reducing capital expenditure for 2026 to the lower end of the guidance range, while continuing to drive down our cost base,” Ms Howle said.

BP last year tore up a strategy, first introduced by former boss Bernard Looney in 2020, to focus on renewables and the green energy transition after a backlash from shareholders. This included scrapping a multi-billion pound hydrogen plant on Teesside.

The oil giant had previously intended to cut its oil output by 40pc and reach net zero emissions by 2050.

The arrival in April of Ms O’Neill, a former executive at US oil major ExxonMobil, is expected to trigger renewed focus on BP’s fossil fuels division.

BP is already increasing its oil and gas investment and exploration. It is also in the process of selling $20bn of assets.

Derren Nathan, an analyst at Hargreaves Lansdowne, said the decision to scrap BP’s share buyback was part of an attempt to “clear the decks” ahead of the arrival of Ms O’Neill as it pursues a “leaner, meaner” strategy.

Shares in BP fell 3.5pc in early trading.

data.title: The French town that puts Britain’s new homes to shame
data.description:
Before she moved to Clamart, Elena Harty had never had much pride in anywhere she had lived.

London had felt too busy and dangerous; Paris too impersonal, its old apartments too poky and temperamental.

But the new town of Clamart Panorama, a former brownfield site just 8km southwest of the French capital, ticks all the boxes.

It has schools, parks, restaurants, a wine bar and a boulangerie all within a 10-minute walk. There are clubs and societies for all age groups; lush green spaces for joggers and dog walkers. It has an abundance of flowers, a network of cycle routes and a direct tram line into Paris.

“We’ve even got a heron,” says Harty, who is 33 and moved from Britain after meeting her partner.

The couple were initially drawn to the area in 2021 by its proximity to the capital and its spacious mid-rise apartments, which imitate the ornate 19th-century mansion blocks that line Paris’s grand boulevards.

In the years since, they have watched Clamart grow into a thriving town of some 5,000 residents. “There are people from really all walks of life,” says Harty.

Affordable housing accounts for 30pc of apartments, but they are mixed in the same blocks as private dwellings, which has given rise to a diverse and close-knit community.

It stands in stark contrast to recent projects in Britain, which have faced criticism for creating drab, low-quality and characterless “ghost towns” full of houses but lacking community and social spaces.

Clamart florist Marie Paolini says her clientele are mainly young professionals and families who have moved from Paris in search of more space. “It’s a really dynamic place,” she says. “We are right next to the tram too, so we get a lot of customers.”

During the day, locals stop and chat between trips to the café and cheese shop, while at night the tables at Bistrot Papa Pool and pizzeria La Bella Vita buzz with hungry diners.

Adrien Lorente, who runs Clamart wine bar La Cave Lelay, says it has started to become difficult to get a table without booking ahead. “We are always full,” he says. “I work from 3pm until midnight and we’re full every time.”

Construction of Clamart Panorama began in 2017 as part of plans to relieve pressure on Paris’s housing market by creating new commutable suburbs for workers.

Work is still underway, with completion of its third and final phase expected for early 2027. But it has already started to become a blueprint for new towns across France.

Britons unconvinced on new-builds

It is a far cry from the latest new-build efforts in Britain. Often derided for being soulless, ugly and lacking a civic heart, the UK’s recent developments have struggled to win public approval.

A 2023 survey by the Chartered Institute of Building found that 40pc of Britons think new-build housing lacks character, while a third considers it poor quality.

New towns built in the last 10 years such as Ebbsfleet, in Kent, and Sherford, in Devon, have faced considerable backlash after homes flew up without any of the most basic amenities such as shops and GP surgeries.

The 3,000-home new town Cranbrook, in Devon, earned the nickname “Crimebrook” after an alleged rise in anti-social behaviour among bored teenagers. But other new developments have simply been accused of being eerily quiet or “dystopian”.

It is a concerning trend as Labour pushes forward with plans to build 12 new towns across Britain, as part of its pledge to build 1.5 million homes by 2029.

Ike Ijeh, of think tank Policy Exchange, says problems in Britain lie in its dogmatic approach to density.

Whereas mixed-used apartment blocks of between four and eight storeys are a common feature of European towns and cities, British developers have tended to prioritise low-rise suburban developments. Nearly 80pc of homes across England are a house or a bungalow, according to the latest census data.

“We idealise culturally in this country the idea of a house – far more than the idea of a flat,” he says. “Developers are aware of the old adage: an Englishman’s home is his castle. That really differentiates us from the Continent.”

But low-rise sprawl carries problems for both developers and the people who live there.

Financially, it is an inefficient use of space. For a similar amount of land required for a two-storey house, a developer could build a mid-rise block containing dozens of apartments and sell them off at more affordable prices.

It also encourages a homes-first, amenities-later approach that often leaves the construction of retail and hospitality spaces delayed until certain population thresholds are met. This means early arrivals can find themselves effectively living in a “ghost town” where they are surrounded by nothing but other houses.

In Clamart, on the other hand, shops, restaurants and local services occupy street-level spaces in many of the residential buildings. This is something Guy Courtois, head of urbanist think tank Pour une Renaissance Urbaine, says is crucial to its success.

“Single houses wouldn’t work. You need density,” he says. “If you want to have a lively town you need to have shops at a floor level, beautiful architecture, parks and greenery. We have all those things here.”

Where mid-rise blocks dominate, easy access to nature is fundamental. In Clamart, balconied apartments are turned towards an expansive central lake, which helps provide a constant sense of the outdoors.

Ijeh says that because mid-rise buildings achieve higher density using less space, developers tend to be able to build in more green space.

“Mid-rise is so efficient at providing density it means there’s more land available for landscaping, compared to what you would have in a low-rise and to a degree in high-rise scenario where you need lots of areas around tall buildings,” he says.

The tram, which takes just 20 minutes to travel into Paris, also plays a crucial role in making the area attractive to investors.

“Bus routes can be changed or cancelled,” says Eleanor Broad, at Create Streets think tank. “Whereas a tram line gives developers confidence about investing in the area.”

‘The key for success is a good vision from a mayor’

Clamart Panorama was not the first development in France to adopt new standards of urban planning.

Just a few kilometres down the road is Le-Plessis Robinson, a much larger town of 30,000, which laid the foundations for Clamart back in the late 1980s.

Swathes of the town’s post-war concrete tower blocks were pulled down and replaced with buildings in the neo-traditional style after new mayor Philippe Pemezec pledged to reverse the area’s decline.

Putting beauty and community at the centre of the project, Pemezec’s vision was a success. Its population rebounded, retail vacancy rates improved and visitors from around the world flocked to see the town’s transformation.

However, like Clamart, it hinged on a factor distinct to France: the authority of city mayors.

In Britain, the majority of urban planning decisions are made by elected planning committees. In France, the responsibility falls to one of the country’s more than 34,000 local mayors, who wield significant influence and control over developments in their commune.

“The number one factor for success is a good vision from a mayor, because they have the power to block any project,” says Courtois.

This means city halls can acquire land at relatively low prices, helping to keep down the overall cost of projects and support the execution of more ambitious ideas.

“Otherwise the mayor can just say, ‘If you don’t want to sell it to me, you’ll do nothing with the land – because I will block anything else’,” says Courtois.

When the city hall bought the land to build Clamart Panorama, it was a brownfield site owned by energy giant EDF. The city hall paid €50m and auctioned off lots to developers for a total of €100m. This gave them €50m to reinvest back into local services.

Watering down the original masterplan is also a lot more difficult than in Britain, where arguing about the cost or speed of construction can often persuade local authorities to loosen design codes. In France, a mayor with a strong vision can simply tell the developer that unless they follow the masterplan to the letter, they will have to sell their lot to a company that can see it through.

Courtois estimates that less than 1pc of new-build projects in France adhere to the neo-traditional principles of Clamart or Le Plessis-Robinson, despite costing just 10 to 15pc more to construct than modern developments.

But the developments are not short of detractors. Neo-traditional architectural styles continue to be dogged by accusations of being pastiche and of creating “toy towns”, and Clamart is not immune to such criticism.

Although the town has a similar look to the 19th-century buildings in Paris, some say the modern methods of using concrete frames and reconstituted stone facades appear less sturdy than Baron Haussmann’s load-bearing limestone structures. The materials also tend to weather differently to real stone, which can make buildings look shabbier over time, rather than adding character.

However, Courtois believes such criticism is largely immaterial, particularly when compared to the towns that post-war modernism provided. “Ask the people who live here,” he says. “If they are happy, then it’s a success.”

data.title: Daily Mail owner’s Telegraph takeover faces four-month investigation
data.description:
The proposed takeover of The Telegraph by Lord Rothermere, the Daily Mail owner, will face a four-month initial investigation by regulators over fears it would hand him too much sway over the press.

Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary, confirmed that the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) and Ofcom would have until June 10 to deliver their preliminary assessments of the £500m deal.

The 16-week schedule is double the statutory requirement of 40 working days, albeit there is a pre-notification phase in addition to that. The referral comes more than three weeks after Ms Nandy said she was minded to involve regulators.

The CMA and Ofcom will examine whether the combination of The Telegraph with Lord Rothermere’s existing news portfolio threatens the public interests in a plurality of ownership and a plurality of views. As well as the Mail titles online and in print, he owns the freesheet Metro and the i.

Lord Rothermere has said he plans to invest in The Telegraph’s international growth and backed its editorial leadership. He has argued that the explosion of news sources online means there should be no plurality concerns over a merger.

But in letters to DMGT, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, and to the independent directors currently overseeing the Telegraph, Ms Nandy’s officials said that the digital reach of the proposed combination was grounds for concern. DMGT titles were accessed by 53pc of UK internet users and The Telegraph by 37pc in July last year. They also warned that print remained influential.

The letters said: “This provides grounds to be concerned that the merger has the potential to result in the loss of a significant and distinct voice in the online news market and a concentration of control.”

The officials appeared to clarify an earlier stance from Ms Nandy that there was an “acute” risk of loss of plurality “especially in the UK’s Right-leaning market”, which it is understood to have sparked protests from DMGT that it potentially prejudiced investigations. The latest letter from Ms Nandy’s officials said any political definition of the markets under scrutiny was a matter for regulators.

If regulators identify a threat to the public interest in their initial investigation it is possible that Lord Rothermere could offer concessions, such as legally binding undertakings on the editorial independence of The Telegraph from the Mail titles, or the sale of Metro and the i.

That could avoid a full “phase two” investigation by the CMA, which could result in the takeover being blocked and heavy costs for DMGT and potentially RedBird IMI, the seller. That could take an additional six months and, with administrative interludes, extend the uncertainty into 2027.

RedBird IMI is a collaboration between the United Arab Emirates and the US private equity firm RedBird Capital. Its own attempt to gain control of The Telegraph was blocked nearly two years ago following a cross-party outcry and legislation banning foreign state ownership.

It has since made repeated faltering attempts to recoup its £500m investment in an onward sale at a price which few investment bankers, media executives or analysts can justify.

However, Lord Rothermere has made two previous attempts to buy The Telegraph and DMGT believes it is uniquely positioned to extract cost savings from back office and commercial functions that will underpin its investment.

The initial “phase one” investigation of Lord Rothermere’s bid may not end until after the three-year anniversary of the beginning of The Telegraph’s ownership saga. In early June 2023, Lloyds Banking Group seized control of the company from the Barclay family over their unpaid debt of £1.2bn.

RedBird IMI and the UAE derailed a planned auction by paying off the debt in a complex transaction intended to deliver them control of The Telegraph. Instead, Redbird IMI has been barred from exercising influence despite having its money tied up in the company.

The leisurely regulatory schedule appears to signal a change of posture from the Government. In November Ms Nandy said her “particular concerns are that the protracted uncertainty has been detrimental to the stability of the Telegraph and its staff” and spoke of the “need for the period of uncertainty to be ended”.

Lord Rothermere’s proposed takeover is the fifth attempt to secure new ownership and now faces significant additional uncertainty.

The political and market backdrop now includes attempted Labour leadership coups from the Left and a renewed focus on press regulation, as well as a steep downward swing in the valuations of companies viewed as exposed to AI.

The takeover is to be financed by a £400m loan from NatWest and a £100m deferred payment to RedBird IMI, due within two years. Credit ratings agencies have warned that DMGT faces a downgrade as a result of the transaction and the greater exposure to the digital challenges faced by news publishers, which could increase its cost of borrowing.

Despite intervening in the proposed takeover, Ms Nandy has not yet given RedBird IMI permission to transfer ownership of the option it holds over The Telegraph to DMGT, meaning no money has yet changed hands.

Ms Nandy’s officials said she was “keenly aware of the financial difficulties facing the newspaper industry”.

A spokesman for DMGT said:“We welcome the Secretary of State’s decision to issue a public interest intervention notice, which marks a significant step forward in our proposed acquisition of The Telegraph.

“As the SoS has recognised, the news media industry is changing rapidly, and publishers must compete with an array of online sources. Against this backdrop, trusted news organisations will play an ever more critical role, and that requires publishers with the resources and experience to compete.  

“We remain committed to investing in The Telegraph and its journalists, preserving its distinctive editorial voice and team, and accelerating its global expansion, with a focus on the US. 

“We look forward to working constructively with regulators and the government to secure a timely completion of the transaction, which will provide stability and certainty to The Telegraph after a protracted period of uncertainty.”

data.title: The banks are booming — one guess what Reeves will do next
data.description:
Profits at NatWest and Lloyds are up. Barclays is doing well enough to reward its chief executive with a £15m pay package. Spain’s Santander is making more money than ever from its British operations.

It is great to see at least one sector of the British economy doing well. There is just one problem. As the Government shifts Left, it will be impossible to resist another raid on the sector – even though it will damage the whole economy.

The economy may have stagnated and business confidence may have fallen to record lows, but at least the high street banks are doing better than ever.

NatWest confirmed the revival on Friday when it reported annual profits up by 25pc at £7.7bn, capping an earnings season that has seen all the major British lenders report excellent results.

It is not hard to understand why all the major banks are reporting such strong profits. Interest rates are positive, households are so worried about their finances that savings are surging, and the property market is resilient.

Small businesses may be struggling to cope with a blizzard of tax rises imposed on them by a Government that doesn’t understand how hard it can be to keep a company afloat, but we have not yet seen a full-scale recession or the kind of mass closures that trigger huge losses for the lenders.

Against that backdrop, the banks should be making money. And they are.

The problem is that this is also going to make them an obvious target for the Government.

Over the past week, with Sir Keir Starmer clinging desperately to power, it has become painfully clear that Labour is going to shift significantly to the Left. It has nowhere else to go.

The backbenchers will demand it, and the main challengers to the Prime Minister, from Angela Rayner to Andy Burnham, are all positioned to Sir Keir’s left, while the moderate right of the party has been fatally weakened by its foolish association with Peter Mandelson.

At the same time, Rachel Reeves is still desperate for money, both to keep borrowing under control, and to offer the extra welfare spending and pay rises for the public sector that are the only things that Labour MPs and party activists seem to believe matter.

With the economy stagnating and contracting when measured by GDP per capita, the tax rises she has already imposed are going to raise less revenue than the Treasury forecast.

It is not hard to work out what is going to happen next. By the summer, yet another “black hole” will have opened up in the Government’s finances.

With the manifesto pledge not to raise any of the three main taxes still in place, a stealth tax on business will be one of the few ways to raise money.

The conclusion is that a windfall tax on the banks looks grimly inevitable. Indeed, in the run-up to the last Budget, the trade unions and a string of Left-wing think tanks were already arguing for a raid on the financial sector.

This year, the demands will be even louder. And yet that will turn into one more self-inflicted disaster.

Sure, the banks may appear to be making huge profits.

But that doesn’t mean this is the right time to hit them with taxes on top of the 25pc corporation tax they already pay, the 3pc “surcharge” on profits above £100m, along with all the National Insurance contributions they pay for their staff, the rates owed on their branches, and of course all the taxes paid by the businesses they lend to.

In reality, the banking industry still faces plenty of serious challenges. The app-based banks, such as Monzo, are still growing quickly, and their expansion is a direct challenge to the traditional giants on the high street. Artificial intelligence will only accelerate that.

It will take a lot of investment in technology for the old-school banks to hang on to the customers they already have – never mind find new ones.

Their costs are still far too high, and political opposition to closing expensive branches is intense, and it will only get worse as soaring taxes mean almost every kind of business – from shops to cafés – closes down.

The tech giants such as Apple, Amazon and Meta have not managed to crack the finance market yet, but that does not mean they won’t keep trying, and with payment systems on every smartphone they will keep chipping away at the margin.

Unlike retailing or media, banking has proved surprisingly resilient to digital disruption, but it may only be a matter of time.

Perhaps worst of all, the UK may soon face a major recession if the global economy or the stock market crashes, and with a spectacularly incompetent Government doing everything possible to destroy confidence in Britain’s prospects.

The banks will need plenty of investment to deal with all those challenges and to keep some capital in reserve to ride through a recession.

If the Government decides to confiscate all the spare cash they make in the good years, then it won’t be available when times are tougher.

The bumper profits the high street banks are making this year won’t necessarily last.

In reality, Britain needs to get away from the idea that success always has to be punished with extra taxes, or that we can get richer by confiscating wealth instead of creating it. A healthy economy needs a healthy banking industry.

The Chancellor will come under intense pressure over the next few months to impose a windfall levy on the UK’s finance industry.

The headline numbers may even offer some justification for that, even if it hardly stands up to a moment of serious scrutiny.

However, it would be a serious mistake – and the entire British economy will suffer if it happens.

data.title: Amazon sellers face ruin as tech giant threatens to withhold payments
data.description:
Amazon has been accused of hoarding third party sellers’ cash after withholding payouts for parcels delivered to shoppers.

The online shopping giant will next month change payment terms for thousands of merchants on its marketplace, leading to warnings that they face financial hardship or collapse.

The move means Amazon will hold onto tens of thousands of pounds from small businesses selling on the site, which merchants said could force them to borrow money to bridge the gap.

Amazon has millions of independent businesses that sell on the site, with payment and often delivery handled by the company.

In recent years, the company has been gradually introducing tougher payment terms for sellers, moving them on to a “DD+7” model in which they are paid seven days after an item is delivered, rather than when it is ordered.

In 2023, the company delayed the changes for some sellers after an outcry from businesses and pressure from ministers.

However, it is moving the remaining sellers who have not so far been transferred to the new terms on March 19.

Andrew Owen-Price, who has sold on Amazon for 20 years, received a notice acknowledging that his business “may experience a one-off cash flow impact”.

He said the changes amounted to “bully boy tactics” that would tie up between £20,000 and £50,000 in revenue. He said he would have to borrow money or sell the business as a result.

“If we did nothing, we would not be able to pay our suppliers at the end of the month, that would drive my business into the ground,” he said.

Llinos Medi, a Plaid Cymru MP, has written to John Boumphrey, Amazon’s UK country manager, saying the terms amount to “an enforced interest-free loan to Amazon from thousands of UK businesses without prior negotiation or meaningful recourse”.

“This approach raises questions of fairness, proportionality and market dominance,” she said.

New changes

Amazon has been putting new sellers on the terms since 2016, but existing sellers have been moved to the new system over time.

In 2023, the company offered extensions after marketplace sellers warned that the changes would force them to go bust and the Government demanded answers from Amazon.

It informed sellers of the changes last year.

Amazon has told sellers it is making the changes to keep money in reserve so that shoppers can receive refunds when they return items.

Hundreds of thousands of businesses in Britain are believed to sell on Amazon, although only a small minority are still on the old payment terms.

Amazon has said in the past that it is making the changes so that all merchants have the same policy.

Emma Jones, the small business commissioner, said she had been in touch with the company over the changes. “My office continues to engage to ensure that small businesses moving onto the process from March are treated fairly and paid in good time,” she said.

An Amazon spokesman said: “The policy to pay businesses seven days after delivery was introduced for new sellers in 2016 and already covers 95pc of independent businesses selling on Amazon.

“A small number of remaining sellers are due to transition to the policy, which ensures sufficient funds are available to cover product returns or customer claims.”

The spokesman added: “These businesses were notified in 2023 to help them prepare, and we continue to offer support throughout this process.”

data.title: Hinkley Point C’s ‘fish disco’ actually works, researchers find
data.description:
An underwater “fish disco” at Britain’s newest nuclear power station has been mocked as an example of the huge cost and bureaucracy involved with building things in the UK.

But at least the new system, designed to preserve fish species, actually works.

EDF, the developer behind Hinkley Point C plant in Somerset, on Tuesday said trials of the acoustic fish deterrent – underwater speakers that blast out sound to keep fish away, dubbed the “fish disco” – found it was “highly effective”.

Tests at sea and in laboratory tanks by scientists at Swansea University showed that only a single tagged twait shad came within 98 feet of the device when it was switched on, compared to 14 when it was off.

This suggested “an effectiveness of more than 90pc”, EDF said, although further trials will be carried out later this year.

The fish disco forms part of £700m of fish protection measures planned for the nuclear power plant, which EDF said gave the site more marine conservation capability than any comparable site in the world.

The findings by Swansea University were welcomed by activists, who said the results proved that ministers should not water down environmental standards for nuclear power.

This week, a cross-party group of some 60 backbench MPs, including more than 30 from the Labour benches, joined nature charities to warn that any relaxation of standards would trigger “unnecessary and hugely damaging environmental regression”.

However, they are being opposed by pro-growth campaigners and industry experts who have pointed to a government review that found nuclear developers faced a “fragmented” regulatory system that often imposed “disproportionate” demands with little regard to costs or trade-offs.

Hinkley’s cooling pipes will suck in around 132,000 litres of water per second from the Severn Estuary to cool the plant’s two nuclear reactors once they are operational.

According to the Environment Agency, this risks putting more than four million fish at risk of being killed. These include Atlantic salmon, herring, European sea bass and European eels, which are critically endangered.

To mitigate the plant’s impact, EDF said it had spent £700m on fish protection measures, including a special intake pipe mouth costing £500m, a fish recovery and return system for £150m and an “acoustic fish deterrent” costing another £50m.

The French state-owned energy giant said the effectiveness of these measures meant it should not have to also create a 900-acre salt-marsh nearby.

Critics of the fish protection measures at Hinkley have branded them “regulatory madness”, arguing they have added huge costs and delays to the project for disproportionately small benefits.

A review by the economist John Fingleton found the fish protection measures would save “0.083 salmon per year” along with “0.028 sea trout, six river lamprey, 18 Allis shad, and 528 twaite shad”.

Sam Richards, of pro-growth campaign group Britain Remade, said: “Blowing £700m on fish protection for Hinkley Point C, including the now-legendary ‘fish disco’, is indefensible in the extreme.

“This is just the latest piece of research that highlights the huge costs and delays Britain’s planning system and habit regulations foist on major infrastructure projects, for little gain.”

Mr Richards also pointed to findings by Swansea University that salmon identified as at risk in fact avoid the Hinkley site.

Salmon migrating to the Atlantic generally used the main channel “well away from Hinkley Point C”, researchers said. In two years, only two tagged salmon were detected within a kilometre of the intake pipes.

Mr Richards said: “After years of delay and hundreds of millions spent, we’re told fish barely go near the intakes at Hinkley anyway. This isn’t environmentalism, it’s regulatory madness.”

However, Matt Browne, of the Wildlife Trusts, defended the measures and accused opponents of misrepresenting the number of fish saved.

Mr Browne said: “We are happy with the acoustic fish deterrent, combined with the other two measures.

“What we would like to see is for EDF to stop belly-aching. This is a hugely important stretch of water for fish, including protected species, and yet there have been persistent suggestions that somehow this is all unnecessary.”

Some 50,000 endangered eels alone were at risk of being killed each year without the measures, Mr Browne said.

He added: “We say this is good news – it is effective mitigation and an example of the rules working as they should do.”

Ministers are expected to set out plans for simplifying nuclear regulations in the coming weeks.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: London’s listed landlords have been unloved for too long
data.description:
Questor, The Telegraph’s investing column, takes a weekly view of the markets – what is moving them, what lies ahead and how all of this could affect your portfolios and financial goals. 

In the wake of the global financial crisis, the big banks traded at a discount for what seemed like forever. The market had given up on them and the narrative was working strongly against them.

This, of course, did not last forever.

During 2021 and 2024, the gold mining industry became a prime target for takeovers thanks to bargain-basement prices, and many deals were struck at a hefty premium.

This, too, did not last forever.

Today, one of the London Stock Exchange’s least loved sectors has a narrative working strongly against it: hefty discounts, lowly valuations and plenty of takeover interest.

So great is the interest that an £8bn bid by the Swiss Zurich Insurance for Lloyd’s of London syndicate manager Beazley is being drowned out by a paltry £150m deal.

Even though the Zurich bid equates to a 63pc premium to Beazley’s undisturbed, pre-bid share price, this column – and more than a few UK equity fund managers and analysts besides, based on recent conversations – are paying more attention to the smaller deal.

This is the cash-and-stock bid from British Land for Life Science Reit. At around £150m, representing a proposed 21pc takeover premium, the putative transaction is surely not as eye-catching.

But this is just the latest in a string of bids for UK real estate companies, and it may well be time to give the nation’s stock market-listed landlords a little more love.

Property plunge

Over the last five years, only nine of the 38 industrial sectors that comprise the FTSE 350 index have fallen in value. Real estate investment trusts (Reits) and real estate investment and services are two of them, with respective falls of 19pc and 21pc over that span.

(The best performer is aerospace and defence, with a 539pc capital gain, before dividends, followed by banks with a 232pc advance.)

It is easy to explain the wretched showing of Reits.

From an operational point of view, bricks-and-mortar retailers face an ongoing onslaught from online rivals to the detriment of demand for high street sites, while the rise of hybrid working is a threat to demand for offices. Meanwhile, the overall economic outlook remains murky.

The steady increase in interest rates between 2021 and 2023 may have crimped demand, hurt net asset value (Nav) calculations and made Reits’ dividend yields look less attractive relative to cash or Gilts.

These issues are all well known.

Less well known is that the property trade press is starting to report shortages of prime London office space. Interest rates still seem to be on a downward trajectory and Reits as a group trade at a sizeable discount to their latest reported Navs.

The current average discount across the sector is some 23pc, but the range is wide, from around zero, at Primary Health Properties and Sirius Real Estate, to more than 40pc at Unite Group, Workspace and Town Centre Securities.

The range partly reflects different target markets between the Reits – which is also why they require careful research – but the gap between price and value is attracting predators.

Sometimes these are trade buyers, as was the case when Belgium’s Shurgard bought Lok’n’Store in 2024, others are financial buyers, such as when investment group Blackstone snapped up Warehouse Reit in 2025.

There have been 13 bids made for London-listed real estate stocks in the past three years. The average bid premium is 24pc, with the highest north of 40pc. The average discount to historic Nav per share represented by those offers is just under 12pc, a figure much closer to asset value than the current sector valuation.

Mind the gap

Experienced investors know that buying a stock in the hope of a bid is a mug’s game. The real point is to buy well-run, well-financed businesses that have a strong competitive position at a sensible price, and tuck them away for the long term so dividends can be farmed (and if possible reinvested). Any takeover is simply a bonus.

But when the takeover activity is reminiscent of that seen in the gold mining industry during 2021 and 2024, and the lowly valuations also sound like an echo of the big banks, it would be foolish not to pay attention.

Eventually canny management teams started to buy back bank stock.

We are seeing the takeovers but not, intriguingly, the buybacks in the Reit sector. Perhaps Saba’s arrival on the share register of both Life Science Reit and Workspace will prompt further action, of one kind or another.

data.title: Driverless car makes emergency stop after being fooled by advert on bus
data.description:
A driverless car mistook an advertisement on the side of a bus for a group of real people and performed an emergency stop, it has been revealed.

The poster – which was promoting the 2015 spy film The Man from U.N.C.L.E. – featured several actors who were identified by the vehicle’s AI system as pedestrians in the road.

John McDermid, a professor of software engineering at the University of York, revealed details of the incident during a briefing in London to discuss the technology.

This week, Waymo, the US driverless car firm, announced that it would launch a pilot of its robotaxi service in London this year, while Wayve is expected to roll out the service with Uber later in the year.

However, British experts cautioned that the technology was not perfect. They said that during trials, driverless cars struggled to distinguish between real people and images, understand roundabouts, identify rule-breaking by pedestrians, and know when to slow down on a tight curve.

During the briefing at the Science Media Centre, Prof McDermid said: “One of the automated vehicle companies I work with had a situation where their vehicle did a sudden emergency stop because it’s all pedestrians in the road, except they weren’t.

“It was a life-size advert on the side of a bus, but to an AI, it was human beings. That seems very obvious [to us], but actually, to the AI, it’s not.”

Prof McDermid also showed a video of automated car trials in York. He said the vehicles struggled when people made unexpected movements, such as crossing a road while the pedestrian light was red.

He said: “It’s seen that there’s a traffic light, so identified... the hazard, because the light is red. It changes to green, the vehicle is about to move off. But this is York, so the tourists, although the lights change to green, they still walk across the road.

“Computer vision doesn’t understand what it doesn’t have models for in the world; it doesn’t know what a roundabout is.”

However, experts said that automation offered an opportunity to address some of the factors that contribute to road casualties.

More than 1,600 people die on British roads annually, with road crashes one of the most common causes of death among young people.

Under the Automated Vehicles Act, passed in 2024, self-driving vehicles on British roads must achieve a level of safety equivalent to, or higher than, that of careful and competent human drivers.

But researchers said that while humans were very good at recognising the sanctity of life, and largely drove carefully to avoid harm to themselves and others, computer systems did not have such a moral code.

Dr Nick Reed, an independent expert consultant at Reed Mobility, said there was something “fundamentally different” between how AI systems viewed people and how humans did.

He said: “[Humans] understand that there are personal consequences if they get caught breaking the rules.

“These behaviours are inherently human and are based on millions of years of evolution. We can’t have the same confidence over the behaviour of automated vehicles.

“I think there is something fundamentally different between a machine labelling an object as [a] person and us as humans, recognising an individual as being a person, someone who can get hurt, someone who has family, someone who has hopes and dreams.

“We need to be sure that the machines with which we share public space, and the companies that operate them, give us the same level of respect.”

Dr Reed said that even if driverless cars proved to be safer than vehicles driven by humans, there would still be mistakes and accidents, which could be very different from the types of incidents which humans had on the roads.

He said AI models would be constantly updated and improved based on real-life incidents.

“The crash that happens in Manchester on Monday should help to prevent a crash in Telford on Tuesday,” Dr Reed added.

data.title: Ed Miliband plots crackdown on wind farms ‘gaming’ power system
data.description:
Wind farm owners who earn huge payouts from gaming Britain’s energy system face a crackdown under new reforms proposed by power grid bosses.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso) warned on Wednesday that companies were deliberately clogging up Britain’s energy system to boost their own revenues.

This is because wind farms can be paid to switch off when the grid becomes too congested to transport their power, allowing traders to game the system.

Neso, which handles the day-to-day operations of the electricity grid, said it was concerned about the sale of power generated by Scottish wind farms to buyers in the South, even when traders know that grid congestion means the power physically cannot be delivered.

There is currently no penalty for doing this, but it means Neso must constantly intervene to balance the system.

For example, it will pay Scottish wind farms to switch off while also paying another power station in the South to generate replacement electricity.

Under the current model, the wind farm and the power station could also be owned by the same company, allowing that company to distort supply to earn payments.

Neso warned the system had created an incentive for companies “to aggravate the constraint” as this “maximises the market participant’s expected revenue”.

It now wants to force generators to sell power from individual power stations, making it more obvious when the system is being gamed.

It also wants to require generators to settle their plans further in advance to minimise last-minute changes to the system.

Balancing costs, which can easily amount to millions of pounds per day, are ultimately passed on to households through their bills.

The problem threatens to push up household bills in a blow to Ed Miliband’s promise to cut them by £300 a year.

Mr Miliband, the Energy Secretary, last year scrapped reforms that supporters said would have ended such market gaming practices following an intense lobbying campaign by wind farm owners, who argued it would torpedo investment.

The amount spent on balancing the grid hit £2.7bn last year alone and is expected to reach between £5bn and £8bn a year by 2030 without action to tackle the problem, according to Neso.

Neso warned: “The misalignment between the national wholesale price and locational balancing needs, along with the scale and predictability of congestion, presents significant risks of strategic positioning against constraints.

“This risk is more likely to arise if the network becomes more congested.”

RenewableUK, which represents wind farm owners, claimed the problem affected “all types of power generation”.

A Neso report last year said most constraint costs were focused in Scotland, where Britain’s wind farms are most concentrated.

A RenewableUK spokesman added: “The industry is working closely with the Government to ensure that innovative solutions are rolled out at speed, such as new energy storage capacity, to add more flexibility into the system”.

A Department for Energy Security and Net Zero spokesman said: “After years of historic underinvestment, we are delivering the biggest upgrade in Great Britain’s electricity network in decades to minimise constraint costs, delivering clean power by 2030 and helping bring down bills for good.

“We are working closely with Neso, Ofgem and industry to further improve the system as part of our Reformed National Pricing package.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Traffic lights should turn green for cyclists, says Lime bike boss
data.description:
Traffic lights should automatically turn green for cyclists, according to the boss of Lime bikes.

Hal Stevenson, policy director for Lime, claimed it could reduce the number of cyclists ignoring red lights.

Transport for London (TfL) last month unveiled plans to give buses priority at all 3,500 traffic lights they pass in London by 2030.

Mr Stevenson called for the measure to be extended to bikes, saying: “Cyclists shouldn’t be left on red.”

Critics described the suggestion as “farcical”.

Mr Stevenson stated that TfL had extended London’s cycling network from about 90 km (56 miles) in 2016 to 430 km (267 miles) last year, with traffic light changes “the natural next step”.

“Rethinking how traffic lights are timed could be a natural next step in building on that success,” he wrote in an article for MyLondon. “Other leading cycling cities like Copenhagen and Amsterdam show what’s possible.

“Green-wave technology, which rewards cyclists travelling at a safe, consistent speed with a sequence of green lights, has improved safety and rider behaviour while reducing journey times at relatively low cost. It could also help address red-light running.”

He said traffic lights were currently timed “around motor traffic speeds”, which “led to frequent stopping for people on bikes”.

“Cycling now accounts for up to one in five trips in central London, with cycle traffic at peak times exceeding cars,” he added.

“Despite this, signal timings remain set around motor traffic speeds, which can lead to frequent stopping and less smooth journeys for people on bikes.”

Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association, said the policy would be “farcical” because London is “incredibly congested” with traffic “slower than it’s ever been”.

He claimed people who hire bikes are “incentivised” to take “dangerous risks” such as ignoring red lights because the companies’ time-based pricing means it is cheaper if journeys are completed more quickly.

Mr McNamara said he attended road safety meetings alongside members of the “white, middle-class cycling lobby” whose “whole obsession is removing vehicle traffic from the roads”.

He added: “Has it not occurred to them that some people either can’t or don’t want to cycle?

“It doesn’t mean there’s anything wrong with them.”

A recent report by location technology company TomTom found London was the world’s slowest capital city to drive in last year.

It took an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to drive one kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of London last year, according to the analysis.

This was partly blamed on widespread 20mph speed limits.

Reducing speed limits from 30mph to 20mph has been one of Sir Sadiq Khan’s key transport policies as part of his ambition to reduce road casualties.

A spokesman for the mayor said journey times on the TfL road network “remain at similar levels to those in 2019” and “studies have shown 20mph limits and zones may improve traffic flow and cut traffic jams”.

A TfL spokesman said: “We already take cyclists, as well as pedestrians and other traffic, into account when we review the timings of around 1,200 signals each year.

“We’re also working to transform the technology behind London’s traffic signals, moving towards an intelligent adaptive traffic signal system that optimises the amount of green time for everyone.”

data.title: Murdoch to take over printing of The Guardian
data.description:
Rupert Murdoch will take over printing The Guardian after the newspaper’s current printer closes down most of its production sites.

The newspaper is currently printed by Reach, which owns titles including the Mirror and Express. However, Reach this week outlined plans to shutter two of its three remaining print sites in an effort to cut costs.

The overhaul means that the majority of Guardian newspapers will now be printed by Newsprinters, a joint venture between Mr Murdoch’s News UK and Daily Mail publisher DMGT, at its site in Broxbourne, Hertfordshire.

A smaller number of copies that are currently printed at the Reach site in Glasgow will be transferred to Scottish publisher DC Thomson.

The transfer, which is due to be completed by July under a 10-year outsourcing agreement, puts The Guardian’s print operations under the control of one of its most deep-seated ideological opponents.

The enmity between the two publishers dates back to The Guardian’s key role in uncovering phone hacking at Mr Murdoch’s News of the World.

The Guardian’s reporting, led by Nick Davies, exposed the widespread interception of voicemails and police bribery by journalists at the tabloid.

The fallout led to the closure of the News of the World in 2011 as well as the high-profile Leveson inquiry into press ethics.

Mr Murdoch’s empire has paid out more than £1bn to settle claims and legal expenses relating to phone hacking.

The saga was dramatised last year in The Hack, an ITV series starring David Tennant and Toby Jones.

The Guardian has also attacked Mr Murdoch and his grip on the British media in its own pages. News UK owns The Sun and The Times as well as broadcasters including Virgin Radio and Talk.

Opinion columns in the Left-leaning newspaper have previously accused the nonagenarian media mogul of “brainwashing” Britain and leaving a “toxic legacy” that gave rise to Donald Trump and the alt-Right.

The closure of Reach’s print sites comes as the news industry adapts to a sharp decline in newspaper circulation as audiences shift online. This has been compounded by the surging cost of newsprint and other inflationary pressures.

Piers North, the chief executive of Reach, said in a message to staff earlier this week: “Given the current customer demand for printed products across the UK and trajectory for the future, I believe now is the right time to consolidate our printing work, to continue to serve our customers and deliver on our strategy.”

In 2024, News UK and DMGT were granted approval by competition regulators to merge their printing operations into a single company.

At the time of the deal, the publishers said the combined company would print on average over three million newspapers each night and almost 22 million per week. The Telegraph is also printed under contract by Newsprinters.

A source close to The Guardian stressed that its contract remained with Reach despite the deal to outsource to Newsprinters.

The Guardian pursued a costly print overhaul in 2005 when it switched to the “Berliner” format. It spent £80m to build specially commissioned new printing presses but later ditched the format and went for tabloid sizing.
