data.title: Baroness Lawrence’s hacking claims ‘wounding’, says ex-Mail editor
data.description:
Paul Dacre, the former editor of the Daily Mail, has described Baroness Lawrence’s allegations of criminality at the newspaper as “bewildering and bitterly wounding”.

Mr Dacre, 77, said the suggestion that his high-profile campaign to bring her son Stephen’s killers to justice was driven by a desire to boost sales and make a profit was “sickeningly misplaced and bleakly cynical”.

Baroness Lawrence is among seven high-profile claimants suing Associated Newspapers Limited for breach of privacy, having been alerted to her potential claim by the Duke of Sussex.

She has alleged that she was “extensively targeted” by private investigators who bugged her phone and put her under surveillance on behalf of the Mail.

Stephen Lawrence, an 18-year-old aspiring architect, was stabbed to death by a group of up to six white youths as he waited at a bus stop in Eltham, south-east London, in April 1993.

The Daily Mail, under the editorship of Mr Dacre, was at the forefront of the fight to bring his killers to justice, branding the five men – Gary Dobson, Neil Acourt, Jamie Acourt, Luke Knight, and David Norris – “murderers” in February 1997 and challenging them to sue for libel.

Mr Dacre told the court on Tuesday: “Throughout my 26-year editorship, this, of all my countless campaigns, many of which made a significant contribution to the public weal, is the campaign of which I am most proud and to which I devoted the most space.”

Mr Dacre, now the editor-in-chief of DMG Media, the holding company of Associated Newspapers, was the first witness for the defence to give evidence.

The judge had previously been told that it was considered “critically important” he went “out over the top first” to address the allegations before sending the “troops into battle”.

Mr Dacre, speaking so softly that he was barely audible, told the court that while he “clearly” wished to clear his name, he was more concerned about the reputation of the Daily Mail and its “honest and dedicated” staff, for whom he said he was “very angry and upset”.

“The grave and sometimes preposterous allegations made in these proceedings have astonished, appalled and – in the small hours of the night – reduced me to rage,” he added in his 52-page witness statement.

Challenged about his lack of acknowledgement of the impact on the claimants, he replied: “My heart bleeds for Baroness Doreen Lawrence, but these are allegations at the moment.”

Mr Dacre said he “utterly rejects” that the Mail hacked and tapped phones, adding that he had “never” placed a story in the Daily Mail as a result of phone hacking and knew of no cases of phone hacking at the Mail.

Such “blatant illegality” would not have been countenanced at any level, and it would have been “insanity” to risk damaging his reputation by allowing such activity to take place, he added.

David Sherborne, the barrister acting for all seven claimants, has repeatedly suggested that Mr Dacre gave false information to the 2011-12 Leveson Inquiry about his newspaper’s use of private investigators and in his insistence that there was “no phone hacking on the Daily Mail”.

Prince Harry told the court he could remember Mr Dacre “clearly stating that phone hacking had not taken place at Associated” and was “so convinced that I didn’t even think twice about it”.

Mr Dacre “emphatically” denied that he had lied to the inquiry about his ban on using private investigators, phone hacking, computer hacking and paying police officers.

He explained to the court that the emergence of private investigators at the turn of the millennium presented a “totally new landscape for journalism” and that most journalists had an “extremely hazy understanding” of the ramifications of the new digital technology and data protection laws.

However, he said he had no recollection of being told of any particular instances or allegations of ”blagging” by journalists or “inquiry agents” during his time as editor and would have been “pretty furious” if significant private information had been blagged that was not in the public interest.

After almost two hours of cross-examination, Mr Justice Nicklin warned Mr Sherborne that the bulk of his questions to Mr Dacre had not addressed the specific claims and said he was “struggling” to understand their relevance.

He warned that the case was not a public inquiry and gave him until 3pm on Wednesday to complete his cross-examination.

Associated is a subsidiary of Daily Mail and General Trust, Lord Rothermere’s holding company, which has agreed to acquire The Telegraph for £500m. The proposed takeover is expected to face regulatory scrutiny in the coming weeks.

The trial continues.

data.title: How mobile phone providers are getting away with 22pc price rises
data.description:
Have you been hit by a mid-contract price increase on your phone bill? Get in touch with us at money@telegraph.co.uk*.

Mobile phone network providers have been accused of “milking” customers with mid-contract price rises as high as 22pc.

Last year regulator Ofcom banned phone providers from including inflation-linked or percentage-based price rises in new contracts over concerns that the practice left customers with uncertainty about the level of future increases.

Customer contracts must now state at the point of sale what any future price rises will be in pounds and pence.

But consumer experts have warned that the new rules have resulted in even bigger increases for customers compared to when bills typically rose by inflation plus 3.9pc.

One O2 customer told The Telegraph that his phone bill rose mid-contract by 22pc last April, after the provider increased prices by £1.80 across the board. This far outstripped the 7.3pc increase he would have seen under the old system.

Consumer group Which? has warned that customers on cheaper tariffs could be disproportionately impacted by fixed uplifts, as they would likely see larger percentage increases to their bill. For example, a £2.50 rise for a £10 tariff amounts to a 25pc uplift, whereas the same £2.50 increase on a £30 tariff equates to a rise of just 8pc.

Martin Lewis, the Money Saving Expert, previously said that Ofcom’s new rules were “flaccid” and that they “effectively embed” above-inflation mid-contract price rises across the industry.

He added: “I don’t understand why we don’t grasp what is by far the simplest and most effective solution: ban firms from increasing prices above inflation during a fixed-term contract.”

Britain’s biggest mobile phone providers – Vodafone and Three (which recently merged), Sky, Virgin Media, O2 and EE (which is a part of BT) – signed a new consumer charter on Wednesday that the Government has said will prevent surprise phone bill increases. However, the policy change still won’t ban mid-term above-inflation increases.

The agreement was in part drawn up in response to an announcement by O2 last year that it would increase bills by £2.50 from April 2026, more than the £1.80 customers had signed up to.

Ofcom said at the time that the uplift went against the spirit of its new rules, which the provider was able to circumvent by allowing customers to leave mid-contract without a penalty within 30 days.

Firms such as Sky include “variable pricing” structures in tariffs which are allowed because the customer has a right to leave within 30 days of being notified.

New consumer rights have ‘backfired’

Business consultant Andy Reed was among those stung by a mid-contract price rise last April.

The 64-year-old, an O2 customer, initially signed up to a one-year SIM-only tariff at £7.99 a month in July 2024. Nine months later, his bill was increased to £9.79, a 22pc rise.

He said: “The fact that my bill has gone up by 22pc in less than a year is frankly outrageous.

“Ofcom’s new rules are simpler but it means operators can exploit it to make more money. People know what their new bill is going to be, but when you look at it in percentage terms, it’s the operators milking their lowest-paying customers. It’s backfired.”

An O2 spokesman said: “An annual rise of £2.50 a month – around 8p a day – continues to represent excellent value for services that customers are using more than ever before.

“We’ve again frozen prices on handset repayment plans and are investing £700m into our mobile network this year to ensure we meet growing demand and give our customers the fast and reliable connectivity they rely on.”

‘Customers ripped off’

All four of Britain’s largest mobile phone network providers are expected to raise prices for most customers under contract by a fixed amount this spring.

The exact rise can vary depending on when you signed your contract, which provider you use and how much data you pay for. Vodafone, Three and EE increase prices in line with what customers agreed to when they signed their contract.

Dr Luke Evans, a Conservative MP, said: “It’s more than discouraging to hear, after Ofcom introduced rules to address this, that some increases are at a higher percentage than when increases were set to inflation plus 3.9pc. It’s no wonder that customers, who are tied into a contract, feel they are being completely ripped off.”

The Telegraph has seen the bill of one customer who is under contract with Three which will rise from £7 to £8.25 in April, representing a 17pc increase. One EE customer’s bill is poised to increase by 9pc to £28.80 in March.

Ernest Doku, of comparison website Uswitch, said: “The best defence is to keep track of your contract end date as switching the moment you’re free allows you to reset your bill and avoid these increases compounding on top of expensive out-of-contract rates.”

Citizens Advice has now called for an outright ban on mid-contract price rises.

The charity’s Anne Pardoe said: “When inflation was at an all-time high, Ofcom was right to act to protect consumers from unpredictable, inflation-linked telecoms price hikes mid-contract. But what started as a well-intentioned reform has left many people facing bigger price rises.

“By focusing on pricing transparency from telecom providers, the regulator has inadvertently made the affordability problem worse. Greater clarity shouldn’t come at greater cost.”

An Ofcom spokesman said: “These commitments build on the significant protections already in place in our competitive telecoms markets – from simpler switching and social tariffs to warning customers when contracts are ending and protecting people from volatile and unpredictable inflation.

“At the same time, broadband and mobile companies are investing heavily in upgrading and expanding their networks, delivering greater choice and better quality while average prices have been falling in real terms.

A VodafoneThree spokesman said: “To give customers greater certainty and transparency, in line with Ofcom’s guidelines, we moved to a pounds-and-pence approach where any mid-contract price rise is clearly set out upfront at the point of sale, so customers know exactly what they will pay across their contract term, and that fixed amount does not change during the contract.”

An EE spokesman said: “We remain committed to Ofcom’s requirement to display upfront pounds-and-pence charges across the full length of a customer’s contract. We will be contacting every customer to explain what price change means for them and the options available. We are not adjusting the annual price change for customers within the minimum term of their contract.”

data.title: British boss of Wall Street bank to get £30m after pay rise
data.description:
The British-born chief executive of US bank Citi has seen her pay rise to $42m (£31m), elevating her into the ranks of the best-paid leaders on Wall Street.

Jane Fraser, who hails from Scotland, saw her total pay climb 22pc in 2025, Citi said on Thursday night.

Her total $42m package ranks her compensation just below that of Jamie Dimon, the JP Morgan boss who earned $43m, and David Solomon, the Goldman Sachs boss who was awarded $47m.

The pay rise comes after Ms Fraser was elected chairman of the Wall Street bank alongside her post as chief executive last year.

Her deal also makes her among the best-paid British business executives globally and one of the top earning businesswomen in the world.

Ms Fraser was born in St Andrews, Fife, and has held the post of chief executive of Citi since 2021.

Educated at Cambridge and Harvard, the 58-year-old joined the bank in 2004 after a stint with McKinsey before rising through the ranks to its most senior position.

The Scottish banker took over as chief executive of Citi from Michael Corbat, who had previously said he did not expect to be replaced by a woman. She remains the first and only female chief executive among the biggest banks on Wall Street.

“One of the pieces of advice I was given when I became CEO is [to] ‘have big ears and thick skin’,” Ms Fraser told broadcaster Channel News Asia in 2023. “I strongly believe in excellence and empathy, and empathy to me is the big ears.”

Ms Fraser has overseen job cuts and axed layers of management at Citi, helping to improve performance.

Pay on Wall Street has jumped over the past year after a surge in deal-making and recovery in US stock market listings helped to boost revenues. The prospect of rate cuts by the Federal Reserve and optimism over the artificial intelligence boom have also helped propel bank stocks higher. Citi’s shares surged more than 65pc last year.

At Goldman Sachs, Mr Solomon’s climbed 21pc to $47m last year. Ted Pick, the boss of Morgan Stanley, saw his pay package jump 32pc to $45m after the bank’s shares climbed nearly 45pc over the year.

Ms Fraser is one of only a handful of women to have secured roles at the very top of the world’s biggest banks.

In the UK, Alison Rose was the chief executive of NatWest until 2023. Marianne Lake, a British-American senior executive at JP Morgan, is seen as a leading contender to replace Mr Dimon, its current chief executive.

data.title: Scandal-hit banking quango in row over redundancies
data.description:
A scandal-hit banking quango is embroiled in a row after using controversial redundancy clauses to block former staff from launching legal claims.

Open Banking Limited (OBL), established in 2016 to encourage banks to share data, has attempted to muzzle former employees during a recent round of redundancies by prohibiting them from taking action against the group once they leave.

The redundancy contracts include clauses restricting individuals from pursuing grievances after termination. Former staffers are also prevented from submitting subject access requests to obtain their personal data and from raising complaints with the Information Commissioner’s Office.

It is unclear whether such terms are enforceable, but OBL maintained in a statement that its historic settlement agreements were legal.

It is understood that at least one former employee is proceeding with an employment tribunal claim because of concerns about the way the redundancy process at OBL has been handled.

OBL was founded a decade ago to encourage savers to switch their bank accounts and boost competition among lenders.

It is funded by nine of the biggest banks in the UK, and regulators at the Competition and Markets Authority are tasked with overseeing the venture.

A spokesman for OBL said that it had “updated the terms of our settlement agreements” and was “confident that our approach complies with employment law”.

It is not the first time that the little-known watchdog has found itself under scrutiny.

In 2021, an independent report found a “toxic culture” of bullying and intimidation prevailed at the quango, which led to the resignation of its chairman.

The Telegraph revealed details at the time of the mistreatment of staff at OBL, which included on-the-spot sackings, verbal abuse, sexism and a culture of cocaine use.

A spokesman for OBL said that, since 2022, a “wholesale cultural overhaul has taken place” at the regulator with “full senior leadership change, supported by robust internal governance, processes and procedures”.

The revelation of the redundancy clauses comes as OBL will soon transition to a new entity which will be funded differently and overseen by the Financial Conduct Authority instead.

The big banks that pay for OBL to operate have criticised the cost of funding it in the past, and last year the group’s chairman said the future entity would have a “fairer and more equitable funding model”.

A spokesman for OBL added: “From time to time at OBL, as with all organisations, there is business change. As a consequence, regrettably, there are a small number of redundancies.

“As part of our redundancy process, OBL may offer individuals a voluntary standard settlement agreement. These agreements are entered into on a voluntary basis by both parties as a means of concluding an individual’s employment. We note that the majority of our small number of redundancies are concluded in this manner.”

The CMA declined to comment.

data.title: Thames Water returns to profit thanks to soaring household bills
data.description:
Higher household bills have helped Thames Water return to profit, even as the struggling utility giant saw its debt pile grow to £17.6bn.

Britain’s biggest water supplier posted profits of £414m in the six months to September, compared to losses of £149m over the same period last year.

This swing stemmed from revenues rising by 42pc to hit £1.9bn in the first half of the year, prompted by the regulator’s decision to approve significant increases in utility bills.

However, despite the improved financial performance, Thames Water admitted it had nearly maxed out a £1.5bn loan facility from creditors, while also highlighting a 5pc increase in debt.

It also warned of an increase in customer complaints owing to higher fees, after Ofwat, the water regulator, allowed Thames Water to increase average bills from £436 to £588.

‘Rise in customer complaints’

Chris Weston, the chief executive, said the utility business’s financial performance had “improved with strong revenue growth, driven by the regulated price rise”.

He said the improvement in its finances had allowed it to spend more on fixing leaks, pollution and water quality, with capital investment up by 22pc to nearly £1.3bn.

He said: “This investment has been funded by higher customer bills, which in turn have led to a rise in customer complaints.”

It comes as the company battles to avoid nationalisation, struggling under the weight of its £17.6bn debt pile.

A group of the company’s most senior creditors are currently pursuing a potential rescue deal, which is deemed one of the last available routes to stop Thames from entering a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

Thames said on Wednesday that its reliance on debt had increased over the six-month period.

However, it said it had also increased investment in clearing blockages, relining sewer networks, repairing leaks and installing acoustic sensors.

Mr Weston said: “This progress has all been achieved as we also manage the recapitalisation of the business.

“We continue to work closely with stakeholders to secure a market-led solution that we believe is in the best interests of our customers and the environment.

“This, in turn, will allow the transformation of Thames to continue, a programme that will take at least a decade to complete and will restore the infrastructure and operations of the company.”

He added: “The first half of this year has been shaped by good progress across all areas of our operational transformation.

“We saw a 20pc drop in pollution, and leakage performance is holding steady despite the extremely dry summer.”

data.title: Killer motorists should be treated like the murderers they are
data.description:
April 25 2007 is etched forever in my mind. It was a beautiful day, and summer was just around the corner. My twin sister had given birth to a baby boy, and my 65-year-old mother, Elizabeth, had come to stay with me in Mortlake, south London. We planned to go to the Chelsea and Westminster Hospital to visit the new baby.

My husband walked my two eldest children, Barnaby and Sapphire – who were six and four at the time – to school and then hopped on a train to work. My mother, my two-year-old daughter, Pollyanna, and I set off to catch the bus to the hospital.

My mother, who lived in rural Shropshire, loved London buses and much preferred them to the train.

We walked, with Pollyanna in her pram, the short distance from our house to the local bus depot. As we approached, Pollyanna climbed out of her buggy and held my mother’s hand.

This was when my life changed irreversibly. I turned around, and all I could see was a monstrous, red Metroline bus approaching at a ferocious speed. Instead of turning into the depot, it drove on to the pavement, aiming straight for us. It happened so fast – almost like a bomb going off.

My legs were trapped under the bus, and I was like the jam in the middle of a horrid sandwich – bus, me, wall. Blood was everywhere. I slipped in and out of consciousness. Out of one eye, I saw my darling, precious, beautiful mother slipping away. She did not survive.

Out of my other eye, I saw my little daughter about 20 ft away. She had been flung through the air, and her right leg was hanging off. I was trapped and could not help her. I shouted for my mother so loudly. I wanted her to answer me, but she did not. She could not.

The crash quickly flooded the news as my husband – The Telegraph’s Christopher Hope – received a call at work to tell him what had happened.

As he made the terrible journey from his office in Victoria to Mortlake, he did not even know if we were alive or not. Four surgeons spent seven hours trying to save Pollyanna’s leg, but they could not, and she had it amputated.

I suffered severe degloving in my leg (where skin and soft tissue is torn from the muscle or bone underneath), among other injuries. However, knowing that I would never see my mother again was the most unbearable wound.

The bus driver who hit us was found guilty of one count of death by dangerous driving and two counts of grievous bodily harm. He was sentenced to four years in prison, which is simply not long enough. Often, only aggravating factors like being under the influence of drink or drugs result in a longer sentence, but it is rare that criminal drivers get more than 12 years behind bars.

On average, four people die every day on UK roads, and many dozens more suffer life-changing injuries. It is no wonder that victims of road crime – like me – feel utterly let down by the UK’s criminal justice system.

Metroline would not speak to me when I rang up their head office. I felt upset, hopeless and angry. So, in February 2014, I set out on a mission – for road crime to be treated as real crime, and for victims and their families to be treated with kindness and compassion in navigating the criminal justice system.

I met Boris Johnson, who was the mayor of London at the time. He said he was very sorry for what had happened to my family. He asked me whether Transport for London (TfL) had apologised, and I said no.

He told me that must change. A few weeks later, my husband and I met with TfL and Metroline. They said they were very sorry. I said that “sorry” must hold meaning, and things began to change from then on.

The Sarah Hope Line was launched in February 2016. It is an incident line that offers confidential support to people involved in, or affected by, life-changing incidents on the TfL network. We have provided to more than 500 families what TfL did not give to us: trauma counselling, assistance with funeral costs and arrangements, and short-term accommodation for family members. Next Monday will mark our 10-year anniversary.

While the line supports families of the bereaved, we still have not yet changed the attitude within government that road crashes are “just one of those things”. This Government should spend much more on policing our roads, so that criminal drivers can be caught. We need to issue immediate fines and driving bans so motorists are deterred from driving dangerously.

Road crime and the damage it causes cost the taxpayer billions of pounds every year – it strains the NHS, criminal justice system and our emergency services. Fatalities, alone, cost the NHS billions every year. That money should be spent on policing our roads and preventing such devastation.

Perpetrators think they can get away with causing death by dangerous driving because sentences do not reflect the severity of the crime. Change must start now. We must no longer be the forgotten victims.

The Sarah Hope Line is on 0343 222 5678, Monday to Friday, 8am-6pm. For more details, go to https://tfl.gov.uk/travel-information/safety/incident-support-service

data.title: BrewDog seeks rescuer as losses mount
data.description:
BrewDog has put itself up for sale and is seeking a rescuer as it grapples with rising taxes and years of heavy losses.

The Scottish brewer, which is one of the country’s best-known craft beer brands, has appointed restructuring experts AlixPartners to sound out possible bidders. A quick-fire deadline for indicative offers is understood to have been set.

BrewDog said it was reviewing its options in light of the “challenging economic climate”. A source close to the company said rising taxes on both alcohol and pubs had been a “massive headwind for some time”.

While sales of its beer to pubs, restaurants and supermarkets make up the majority of BrewDog’s business, the company also has dozens of bars in the UK.

Alcohol duty rose this month and pubs and bars face a steep increase in business rates under plans set out by Rachel Reeves last month. Both impact BrewDog.

The Telegraph has been campaigning to lessen the tax burden on the pub sector amid warnings of widespread closures without more support.

While the recent tax changes have put pressure on the craft brewer, BrewDog has been loss-making for years. In 2024-25, the company lost £37m on a turnover of £357m.

Sales growth has also slowed markedly amid a cost of living squeeze and as younger generations drink less, or abstain from alcohol altogether.

Many of those who are drinking are turning away from the strong flavours of craft beer, with Guinness and lager Madri performing particularly well recently. Last year The Telegraph reported that BrewDog’s beers had been axed by almost 2,000 pubs across Britain.

Brewers must also contend with high energy prices. Record numbers shut down last year and Yorkshire-based Black Sheep Brewery narrowly avoided collapse last month after managing to strike a rescue deal.

Possible suitors for BrewDog are likely to include Heineken, Asahi and ABInBev, the brewing giant behind Budweiser and Stella Artois. Carlsberg may also examine the business.

James Watt, BrewDog’s controversial co-founder, is also considering an offer for the company according to industry press. Mr Watt, who owns a 21pc stake in the company, ran BrewDog until stepping down in 2024 in the wake of allegations he created a “culture of fear”. He denied the claims.

AlixPartners’ appointment, which was first reported by Sky News, may pave the way for a break-up, with the brewing operations in Ellon, Scotland, the US, Australia and Germany hived off from the company’s 72 bars worldwide.

The decision to kick-start a sale process this week comes as TSG Consumer Partners (TSG), BrewDog’s private equity backers, seek an exit from an investment held for almost 10 years. TSG has representation on the BrewDog board, which decided to appoint the restructuring experts.

The US private equity group, based in San Francisco, invested £213m in BrewDog in 2017 in an unorthodox deal which turned the brewer’s founders into multimillionaires. The deal, which handed TSG a 21pc stake, valued the craft beer company at £895m.

Unusually, the private equity company insisted on a clause guaranteeing an 18pc compounding rate of return on its investment. 
The arrangement has effectively saddled the business with a debt to TSG of more than £800m, which may act as a deterrent for any would-be buyers.

Any sale is likely to see the thousands of small-time investors who backed BrewDog through its “Equity for Punks” crowdfunding get little or nothing for their investment.

Between 2009 and 2021, BrewDog raised £73m from 220,000 individual shareholders, with the average investor putting in £400. The most recent crowdfunding round valued the brewer at £2bn, but any sale is expected to be well below that price.

Over the past year, BrewDog has closed more than a dozen bars, cut jobs and sold a Scottish forest meant to bolster its net zero credentials as it scrambled to improve its finances.

Its downturn is in sharp contrast to its wild ascendancy, in which it won the support of a legion of millennial drinkers with its Punk IPA and rode the wave of demand for craft beer.

Mr Watt and business partner Martin Dickie founded BrewDog in Fraserburgh, Scotland, in 2007 and grew it into Britain’s biggest independent craft brewery, buoyed by marketing stunts that included driving a tank through central London and bottling a 55pc beer in embalmed stoat and squirrel carcasses.

A BrewDog spokesman said: “As with many businesses operating in a challenging economic climate and facing sustained macro headwinds, we regularly review our options with a focus on the long-term strength and sustainability of the company.

“This is a deliberate and disciplined step with a focus on strengthening the long-term future of the BrewDog brand and its operations.”

BrewDog said it remains “the number one independent brewer in the UK” and is confident its reputation will attract “substantial interest”.

“Our breweries, bars, and venues continue to operate as normal. We will not comment on any further speculation,” a spokesman said.
